sentences.text: My name is Elizabeth Mann.
My maiden name is Elizabeth Mohr.
M-O-H-R.
1925, December.
Kecskemet.
That is in Hungary.
OK, my father's name was Josef and my mother's name is Charlotte Mohr.
And I had an older brother, Imra, an older sister, Claire, and a younger brother, Laci, and myself.
Yes, I remember very well my childhood.
It was a very happy childhood.
And my father had a hobby.
He played the violin very beautifully.
My mother had a gorgeous voice, so singing was always in our house.
And my father had a dream that if he had his own orchestra of his four children.
So we all four started different kinds of musical instruments to make  up the orchestra.
A piano.
But since the war, I never played.
Every time my parents' friends, or, excuse me, my friends came over to our house, my father took the violin forward and said, come on, let's sing.
So we were singing a lot.
That time when nowhere TV or radio was something, but didn't have any program actually on-- just two hours in every day, altogether.
So you did entertain yourself, or your friends you did entertain.
So we did play games and singing and doing things like that-- reading even together.
Yes, and if we talk about songs, so many of them-- my father and my mother loved light opera.
Many, many times they did organize, you know, a group from their friends and made the play.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] today, when I hear the same melody, many times I just start to cry, even today.
So many, many of them, you know, what I do  know still hurt me when I hear.
Not in English, no.
I recognize them when they are playing it.
But I-- for example, from the and plays like that.
Mmm-mm.
My father had a business.
And he transported that to Budapest, and from Budapest, they were transporting it to Germany and all over Europe, as far as I know.
That was-- and they were wools-- animal wool and feathers or, you know, things like that-- I don't know what actually-- and leather for shoes, and coats, and things like that.
Yeah, sure, I did go to school.
I wanted to be a teacher.
And I started to go to school.
In Hungary, or in Europe, was a system.
You go into elementary school, naturally, and junior high, and from there, they had actually so-called teacher's college, where you did go to study.
Naturally, 1943, you couldn't go anymore, because you were a  Jew.
But from first year of junior high, my teachers gave me students to tutor.
And I did.
So I had one girlfriend, who were-- she was my best friend also-- was tutored by me.
And she continued the teacher college.
And I was tutoring her.
She was bringing the book, all of them.
And I was reading it, and telling her.
And that way, I was keeping up my studying.
It was ?] a last-minute, really deal.
We did go into the ghetto.
Magda. ?]
No, she was Catholic.
And her father forbid her to come to our house.
He was actually an anti-Semitic person.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she still did come every single day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] even Saturday and Sundays.
I did from 3 years old.
I did go to so-called cheder.
And then I did attend a Hebrew school, because you had to if you did go to junior high or high school.
We did continue our Hebrew studying.
Besides, I did study Hebrew language also.
I-- did write Hebrew and read Hebrew.
But now, I wouldn't be able to write.
In my home?
My home was so-called orthodox.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had kosher.
We didn't do anything with work Saturdays.
But our maid bought the ticket, movie tickets for my parents.
And they did go.
And my father had hair.
You know, he wasn't shaved bald.
I don't know.
How else can I-- we naturally kept all the holidays, but not very strict in the rituals.
I remember all the holidays in our home, because that was always so much fun.
First of all, my parents always invited people, even then.
So our house was full of people.
I loved Passover.
I loved Rosh Hashanah and Sukkot.
Sukkot, the people came over, and my parents also with walnut we played a game-- I don't know if in America they do play-- like bowling.
You know, you lined up the walnut, and from a certain distance, you had to hit the walnut.
Depending on where did you hit the I think 9 or 10 walnuts you lined up, from the hit, you got the walnut-- you know, all of them.
So that was a game.
Or you put it on each other three, and you-- like bowling.
I loved that game, because you could play it hours and hours.
I loved Hanukkah.
We played with American peanuts, what was very big deal in Hungary.
You got it just in that time.
I don't know why.
And we played instead of money, you had 10 peanuts, or whatever.
And you played with it.
You lost or gained.
So many, many holidays had fun.
What was the Jewish population in your town?
In my town?
About 1,600 people.
That were very few compared to 100,000 people living in the city.
So you was known by everyone.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I am not talking about the Jewish people know each other, everyone, because not as friends or something, but by the Christian people.
They did know exactly where the Jews lived.
No, that was not existing in our city.
Everybody lived in sometimes 20 blocks from each other, or I don't even know all the people, where did they live, just the few Jewish people whom we were friend.
Very warm and very good because I did go to school.
They were all Catholic children.
And they came to my house after school.
Or we studied together.
Or we were just singing together or playing together.
I never felt-- that was our tragedy-- that I never felt that I am different from them.
You see?
I don't know if you know what assimilate means-- that we didn't feel differently from the Christians.
My father was a hero in the First World War.
He had all kinds of medals.
He was honored every time.
And he was very proud to be a Hungarian, and so was I.
And matter of fact, my grandfather-- one of my grandfathers-- was a bodyguard for Queen Elizabeth, who was a queen for Hungary and Austria from 1867 to 1918, till the end of the First World War.
And my father was a hero in the First World War.
But all my uncles and ancestors going back 500 years in Hungary also were soldiers in the Hungarian Army and fighting for Hungary.
So I didn't feel that I am not Hungarian.
And that's why that really did hurt tremendously, what happened to the Hungarian Jews in 1944.
Yes.
When, in Germany, 1933, things started to happen against the Jews, we right away didn't know anything what was going on.
First of all, there was a censor all the time in-- in Hungary.
Second, there was no TV, no radio-- had just a few people.
So the news traveled very, very slow.
And when you did hear something, that never came to the newspaper, naturally.
So in Hungary, the anti-Semitism, even if they had any, I didn't feel it.
I was part of the population, just.
In 1939, when the war started, then you started to feel, because Germany and Hungary became allies.

And that meant the German soldiers, tanks, and everything else was allowed to come into Hungary and stay   in Hungary and from Hungary go into the surrounding other countries, because they did go, from 1939, around Hungary and occupied other countries.
So that was very convenient for the Germans to use Hungary as a center for the whole operation.
With the soldiers, naturally, came a lot of hatred into-- into the country.
But when someone suggested to my father that we should leave the country, my father said, why?
I am Hungarian.
Where would I go?
And why would I go?
Nothing can happen here to me.
He was so sure.
And 1941, my brother was drafted.
So not only my father was in the First World War, but my brother also became a Hungarian soldier.
And that's called in Hungary an exceptional group, you know, when you serve the country.
That was exceptional, the soldiers, because they were horrible to them, but just to tell you how that was in Hungary.
So naturally, slowly, everything got worse.
But we still believed that we are Hungarians.
And eventually the Germans will leave the country because they will lose the war.
Everything will be back to normal.
Did you ever at any point feel that you should leave the country?
No.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was one time a rumor, I don't know which year, that the German regiment-- not regiment.
I don't know Hitler or who had the idea-- that all the European Jews will go to Madagascar.
He wanted to transport them to Madagascar.
That we did hear.
But naturally, we didn't believe it.
Then, everybody tried to go and learn some kind of a trade.
So if we were transported, we should be able to support ourselves.
But naturally, that never happened.
That was just rumors.
Yes, we all did.
But--
Oh, I started to learn how to sew.
But I never did learn how to sew by the end.
We were a very close family, not only with my sisters and two brothers.
Matter of fact, I think we were too close.
But my cousins and aunts, uncles, also, because in the same city, there was five-- four brothers and my mother living in the same city. 10 sisters and brothers my mother had.
And all the cousins, we grew up together.
And every single week, at least once, the whole family met by one of the family member, I mean, or by my mother's house, or by my uncle's, one of the uncles.
And even weekdays, we met with the family.
So the relationship was very, very close.
No.
Oh, oh, our maid.
Yes.
But I thought you were talking about relatives or somebody.
No, that's OK.
Yes, we had a maid more than 16 years.
She lived with us.
And she called my parents the good [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lord, or not the God, but you now, I don't know an English expression for that.
And she loved them, really.
Klara.
And she really had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] things, you know, what other maids didn't have.
My mother have her every single year a month's paid vacation, what was unheard of in that time.
And she had freedom in the house.
She did whatever she wanted.
She could go out whenever she wanted, and so on, besides the work, what she knew what she was expected to do.
And as a matter of fact, 1939, when the Hitler era fell in Hungary, she wrote articles to newspapers about the unjust against the Jews, because she said how wonderful the Jewish people were to her.
She was an orphan in the beginning.
And Jewish people raised her.
So our disappointment was unbelievable when the hardship came, and we had to go to the ghetto, that she didn't ever come to help us.
But after the war, when my other brother returned to Hungary, she-- he aslo took her in to the house, just to have vacation for her.
She did work for someone else that time.
So-- and I was sending her packages from Sweden after the war, forgetting what she didn't do during the ghetto time.
So you took care of her after.
Yes, even she wasn't loyal to us, so to say.
Yeah, she was-- he was drafted in 1941 in the Jewish Army.
That's what you are asking.
Once-- once he came home [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for three days.
And I never could see him again, because-- I am cutting way ahead myself-- but after the war, he was taken during the war to the Russian front.
And when the war came almost to the end, he was taken to Yugoslavia by the German SS to work in a copper mine as a Jewish soldier.
And he was between them-- you know, one part of the mine blew up.
And they were taking out the Jewish young soldiers and walked-- marched somewhere.
I don't know the place.
But that is recorded in the history.
And had to dig their own grave, and he was shot into that.
But he was the last few who was shot into their grave.
And when darkness came, he climbed out from the grave and ran away.
A Yugoslav woman found him collapsed, took him in, and nursed him for three days.
My brother himself took out the two bullets of himself with a pen knife.
But German SS men came from house to house in that place looking for food and found my brother there and took him to a concentration camp, to Germany.
So you see, they knew they lost the war, meaning the Germans, but to kill, still, that was the most important thing for them-- to kill the Jews.
So he took him to Germany.
And I don't know the date when, but he was liberated.
And he returned to Hungary looking for us.
But he didn't find anyone.
And then he was asked to join the Communist Party.
And my brother did find out a lot of things what was going on in the city before we were taken away, and-- and so on.
And the very same people who were the greatest Nazis became the greatest communists.
So my brother said he is not joining of them.
One of them said-- later, I tried to take my brother out from Hungary when I finally found out he is alive.
I did go to the Swedish king to ask permission for my brother, visiting permission, to come just to see him once.
And he did the paper.
And then, the Hungarians made him pay a lot of money, luxury, and so on, for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] airplane ticket and everything.
But they told him that you will never leave the Hungary alive.
Then they took him off of the plane.
They let him go so far to sit on the plane.
And then, they took him off and told him you will not leave Hungary ever.
And they kept their word, because later one of the Hungarian communists in my city watched for him.
The irony is that he did go to that few Jews who returned to the city to [SIGHS] [SOBBING] go to the temple to pray for the dead who died in concentration camp.
And as he was going, one of the Hungarian communists hit him with a truck purposely, and he died.
So his grave is in my city.
Mine-- that's not mine anymore.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's what happened to my older brother.
I told you that I had an older sister and a younger brother, also.
Then my-- my sister did work for a Christian company.
She was a buyer.
And three days later, as the Germans occupied Hungary-- that was March 19, 1944-- she was sent to Budapest by her boss.
And she never did return.
The SS-- German SS-- with the Hungarian soldiers' help, or police help, took her off from the train.
And my parents never knew what happened to her, and she never returned.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So after that, very soon came out the new law in Hungary that every Jew has to wear a yellow star and yellow arm band.
I don't know if that was in end of March.
I don't know exactly when.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about, I don't know, 10 days after we were wearing the  yellow star, we had to go to the ghetto.
Between that time, it's not clear anymore for me.
So we did go to the ghetto, the ghetto in-- in my city that was one single house owned by a Jew.
And that was called ghetto.
And another house owned by a Jew, 10 blocks, or 5 blocks, or any blocks away, was the other part of the ghetto.
So the ghetto wasn't in one place.
